behaviors.description: The wait time between retries doubles with each consecutive failure, up to a configured maximum.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is retry/backoff math and cap logic inside local state/algorithm. Behavior text is only about wait-time progression ("doubles" and "up to a configured maximum"), not module handoff, OS timing, or network effects. Rejected higher levels: no real HTTP/subprocess/filesystem requirement is stated, and no AI/ML output is involved.
behaviors.test_description: Test the backoff calculator/state function directly with consecutive failure counts and config (base delay, max delay), asserting exponential growth and max cap behavior.

behaviors.description: The retry logic correctly identifies a rate-limit response and computes the appropriate wait time before the next attempt.
behaviors.justification: This behavior crosses a module boundary: response classification (rate-limit detection) must feed retry/backoff decisioning correctly. Unit is likely too low because a bug can appear in handoff between classifier and retry policy. Rejected System: wording does not require real HTTP transport; in-process stub responses can reproduce the failure. No AI/ML dependence.
behaviors.test_description: Wire retry orchestrator with a stub transport returning a synthetic 429/rate-limit payload (and non-rate-limit controls), then assert classifier output drives the expected computed delay for next attempt.

behaviors.description: After the wait, the client actually resends the original request and returns the successful result to the caller transparently.
behaviors.justification: Requires orchestration across timer/backoff scheduling, request execution, and response propagation modules. A single-function unit test would miss resend + transparent return wiring failures. Rejected System: behavior does not require real network or OS primitives; fake clock + stub transport can expose this failure boundary. No AI/ML dependence.
behaviors.test_description: Using fake timers and a stub API client, make first call return rate-limit then second return success; assert original request is resent after scheduled delay and `call()` resolves with the successful payload without caller-side retry handling.

behaviors.description: After the configured maximum number of retries, the client returns a typed `QuotaExhaustedError` rather than a generic error.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at interaction of retry-counter termination and error-mapping layer; this is more than isolated constructor logic. Rejected System: no real HTTP/OS behavior is necessary to verify typed error after exhausting retries; deterministic stub failures are sufficient. No AI/ML dependence.
behaviors.test_description: Configure low max retries, have stub transport always return rate-limit, run `call()`, and assert final rejection is specifically `QuotaExhaustedError` (type/class and key fields), not a generic error.

behaviors.description: The full retry chain — initial call, backoff retries, quota tracking — terminates correctly and does not loop indefinitely before surfacing the error.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly names cross-module chain (initial call + backoff + quota tracking), so minimum boundary is integrated state-machine/orchestration. Rejected Unit: wording explicitly disqualifies a single-function scope. Rejected System: no text requires real HTTP/network/OS; in-process stubs plus bounded fake time can catch non-termination bugs. No AI/ML dependence.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full in-process retry pipeline with always-rate-limited stub responses and fake timers; assert finite number of attempts, eventual terminal error, and explicit guard that promise settles within expected bounded virtual time.

behaviors.description: Requests issued concurrently from multiple async callers in the same process all draw from the same shared quota bucket.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is shared state coordination across concurrent callers and quota manager module, not a single local function. Rejected System: behavior is explicitly "in the same process," so real distributed/network infra is not required; local async concurrency test is sufficient. No AI/ML dependence.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate one client/quota manager, launch many concurrent `call()` invocations against a stub transport, and assert aggregate permit consumption/denials reflect one shared bucket across all callers (not per-caller isolated counters).

behaviors.description: When multiple requests are waiting to be sent, they are dispatched in the order they were originally submitted.
behaviors.justification: Ordering correctness depends on queueing + scheduler + dispatch interaction under contention; this is a module-handoff/state-propagation behavior. Rejected Unit: a pure queue unit test would miss integration bugs where dispatcher violates enqueue order. Rejected System: no real network/OS dependency is required; controlled in-process async test can expose ordering defects. No AI/ML dependence.
behaviors.test_description: Queue multiple requests quickly under throttled conditions with a stub transport and controlled clock; record actual dispatch sequence and assert FIFO matches submission order.
